elements.sent-2411-001: Alexander Spangher was the primary writer, he was the advisor for Michael and Sriya, and he performed the analysis in Section \ref{sct:gameplay_design .}
elements.sent-2411-002: Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated impressive capabilities in generating coherent text but often struggle with grounding language and strategic dialogue.
elements.sent-2411-003: To address this gap, we focus on journalistic interviews, a domain rich in grounding communication and abundant in data.
elements.sent-2411-004: We curate a dataset of 40,000 two-person informational interviews from NPR and CNN, and reveal that LLMs are significantly less likely than human interviewers to use acknowledgements and to pivot to higher-level questions.
elements.sent-2411-005: Realizing that a fundamental deficit exists in multi-turn planning and strategic thinking, we develop a realistic simulated environment, incorporating source personas and persuasive elements, in order to facilitate the development of agents with longer-horizon rewards.
elements.sent-2411-006: Our experiments show that while source LLMs mimic human behavior in information sharing, interviewer LLMs struggle with recognizing when questions are answered and engaging persuasively, leading to suboptimal information extraction across model size and capability.
elements.sent-2411-007: These findings underscore the need for enhancing LLMs' strategic dialogue capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-008: Recent research has shown that LLMs struggle to engage in emotional \cite{shaikh2024grounding or strategic \cite{wongkamjan2024more dialogue.}}
elements.sent-2411-009: For example, \newcite{shaikh2024grounding examined LLM-generated responses to dialogues and found fewer occurrences of ``grounding language''~\cite{clark1996using, cho-may-2020-grounding , like acknowledgements or affirmations, that humans typically use to foster comfort and trust.}}
elements.sent-2411-010: This can impede an LLM's ability to serve in a variety of situations: e.g. education \cite{kasneci2023chatgpt , mental health \cite{carlbring2023new or conflict resolution \cite{argyle2023ai .}}}
elements.sent-2411-011: However, prior efforts to ameliorate such gaps face limitations: existing large datasets (1k-10k transcripts) are generated via crowdsourcing and are inherently unnatural \cite{rashkin-etal-2019-towards, wang2019persuasion,liu2021towards .}
elements.sent-2411-012: More natural transcripts, of educational \cite{caines2020teacher or therapeutic environments \cite{gratch-etal-2014-distress , are difficult to collect due to privacy concerns \cite{casey2004challenges and are small-scale (100-1k transcripts).}}}
elements.sent-2411-013: In this work, we directly address these limitations by focusing on an area where grounding communication is required but plentiful data exist: journalist interviews.
elements.sent-2411-014: Journalistic, or informational interviews, are typically conducted between an ``interviewer'' and a ``source'', and the goal is to obtain information.
elements.sent-2411-015: Sources are often anxious or unclear \cite{harcup2015journalism , and human interviewers are constantly evaluating: (1) Are my questions getting fully addressed? (2) Do I need to more effectively engage or persuade a source \cite{sedorkin2015interviewing ? This makes news interviews an ideal setting to observe grounding, strategic, and persuasive dialogues.}}
elements.sent-2411-016: To study how LLMs perform in journalistic contexts, we start by collecting interview transcripts from two major news sources: National Public Radio (NPR) and Cable News Network (CNN), filtering to over 40,000 dyadic informational interviews.
elements.sent-2411-017: Next, we show that LLMs in news interview settings suffer from the same lack of grounding as in other dialogue settings \cite{shaikh2024grounding .}
elements.sent-2411-018: We find that significant discourse differences exist in the kinds of questions asked by LLMs and human interviewers: for example, LLMs are 50\% less likely to make acknowledgements, and 30\% less likely to pivot back to higher-level questions than humans.
elements.sent-2411-019: Next, we turn to a more fundamental question: why grounding? According to \newcite{cialdini2009influence , grounding exists for a purpose: to influence an outcome (e.g. in therapeutic environments, the grounded patient is more open and makes more progress \cite{bohart1999clients ; in educational environments, the comfortable student is more engaged and learns more \cite{brown1994guided ; these are all usually borne out over many conversational turns).}}}
elements.sent-2411-020: Observing the effects of grounding language in terms of objective outcomes might be a more effective way to reason and ultimately train empathetic agents and improve long-horizon strategic dialogue.
elements.sent-2411-021: Motivated by these observations, we develop a realistic game environment to serve as a playground: in this simulation, LLMs play the role of the interviewer and the source.
elements.sent-2411-022: The goal for the interviewer is to obtain the maximal amount of information from the source in a limited number of questions.
elements.sent-2411-023: In order to induce the need for grounding communication, we design different personas for sources (e.g., anxious, clueless, dominating), each with different communication patterns.
elements.sent-2411-024: We also add a responsiveness to strategic dialogue: sources will only return information if they are persuaded in a manner befitting their personas\footnote{We understand that ``being persuaded'', ``being made comfortable'' and ``being acknowledged'' are all separate forms of grounding, some more active than others.}
elements.sent-2411-025: However, we use ``persuasion'' as a short-hand encompassing all categories.. \cite{harcup2015journalism, sedorkin2015interviewing .}
elements.sent-2411-026: We find that our environment is realistic: source-LLMs correlate significantly with humans in their ability to identify persuasion ($r=.43$, $p<.0001$).
elements.sent-2411-027: However, interviewer-LLMs struggle to both recognize when questions are answered and actively persuade the source, resulting in suboptimal information extraction.
elements.sent-2411-029: We release a high-quality dataset of 40,000 two-person informational interviews from NPR and CNN.
elements.sent-2411-030: This dataset addresses the scarcity of large-scale dialogue data necessary for studying grounding communication.
elements.sent-2411-031: We perform a detailed discourse analysis comparing LLM-generated dialogues with human interviewers, identifying significant differences in the use of grounding language and question types.
elements.sent-2411-032: We develop a game environment to test and improve dialogue agents in informational interviews, which we call NewsInterview.
elements.sent-2411-033: Our findings indicate this is a realistic setting but highlight the challenges LLM interviewers face in engaging in persuasive dialogue.
elements.sent-2411-034: In this section, we describe the collection and processing of our dataset.
elements.figure-2411-001: \begin{subfigure}{\linewidth} \includegraphics[width=.8\linewidth]{figures/question_types_over_time.png} \caption{\% of Discourse types throughout human interviews. Human journalists use different discourse roles across the interview, including gradually more Acknowledging statements, increasing from 5\% at the start to over 20\% by the end.} \label{fig:human-discourse-across-time} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{\linewidth} \includegraphics[width=.8\linewidth]{figures/question_types_over_time_sans_legend__baseline.png} \caption{\% of Discourse types of LLM responses in interviews. LLMs display an increasing likelihood of asking opinion or broadening questions over the course of an interview and a lower likelihood of returning to outline-level questions.} \label{fig:llm-discourse-across-time} \end{subfigure} \caption{Comparison of discourse types across interviews (turn 1, usually a greeting, is excluded). LLM is shown the first $k-1$ turns of a human interview and asked to generate the next question.} \label{fig:comparison-discourse-over-time}
elements.sent-2411-035: We aggregate, clean and condense multiple publicly available datasets of interview transcripts from National Public Radio (NPR) and Cable News Network (CNN) in order to build a high-quality interview dataset of 45k source-to-interview transcripts.
elements.sent-2411-036: These transcripts are published records of live interviews conducted between a journalist and sources invited on the program.
elements.sent-2411-037: They provide a rich resource for analyzing natural language interactions in informational interviews.
elements.sent-2411-038: We want to focus on one-on-one informational interviews between a journalist and a single source.
elements.sent-2411-039: We start with 487,310 transcripts collected by \cite{majumder2020interview, zhu2021mediasum .}
elements.sent-2411-040: However, initial examination of the transcripts reveals many of them to be low-quality: many include multiple sources, are formatted as panel discussions, or are not informational in nature (e.g., they included game shows).
elements.sent-2411-041: To filter the transcripts and retain only those that fit our criteria, we prompt Llama-3.1-70b\footnote{\url{https://huggingface.co/meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3.1-70B-Instruct \cite{touvron2023llama using the vLLM framework \cite{kwon2023efficient to classify each transcript based on the number of participants and the nature of the content.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-042: The prompts used for filtering are provided in App.. \ref{app:prompts .}
elements.sent-2411-044: Finally, the original transcripts do not distinguish which participant was the interviewer vs. the interviewee.
elements.sent-2411-045: So, we count each participant's use of question marks: the participant with more is labeled the interviewer.\footnote{Manual validation on 50 interviews showed this method correctly identified roles in $>98\%$ of cases.}
elements.figure-2411-002: \begin{tabular}{lrrrrrr} Exact Match Info. Motivation Style Discourse Context Baseline-LLM 3.9\% 4.4\% 4.7\% 11.9\% 36.2\% 53.0\% Chain-of-Thought (CoT) 4.5\% 3.6\% 5.2\% 12.8\% 37.0\% 56.9\% LLM w. Outline 3.7\% 3.8\% 4.1\% 9.6\% 36.2\% 46.6\% Outline-CoT 3.6\% 3.9\% 4.3\% 8.3\% 29.9\% 43.1\% Human 8.2\% 17.5\% 35.4\% 40.2\% 54.5\% 60.3\% \end{tabular} \caption{Alignment of LLM-Generated Questions with Original Interview questions. We give an LLM, Llama-3.1-70b, the prior $k-1$ turns in an interview and prompt it to ask the next question. We measure the percentage of times this question aligns to a question asked by a human at the same point in the interview across six dimensions: Exact Match (nearly exactly the same as the original utterance), Information (relevant factual content), Motivation (same motivation as the original question), Style (alignment with tone and phrasing), Discourse (structural role within the interview), and Context (incorporation of contextual knowledge). The prompting strategies compared are Baseline-LLM, Chain-of-Thought (CoT), LLM with an Outline, and Outline-CoT; and, we conduct a human baseline trial with a former professional journalist.} \label{tab:consistency_evaluation}
elements.figure-2411-003: \includegraphics[width=.8\linewidth]{figures/pie_charts_full_4_col.png} \caption{Distribution of Discourse Roles in Questions, Across Different Prompting Strategies. We compare the proportions of discourse roles of questions (e.g. ``Follow-up'', ``Acknowledgement'', etc.) generated by (a) human journalists, (b) Baseline-LLM (Llama-3.1-70b) (c) LLM prompted with an Outline and (d) with Chain-of-Thought (CoT). Acknowledgement statements, which often build empathy, are significantly underrepresented in all LLM prompting approaches, compared to human-generated questions (see appendix for Outline-CoT).} \label{fig:discoure_pie_charts}
elements.sent-2411-046: In this section, we analyze how humans conduct informational interviews and compare this behavior to that of pretrained LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-047: Our aim is to explore whether LLMs face similar grounding problems in interviews as observed in educational and therapeutic settings \cite{clark1996using, shaikh-etal-2024-grounding .}
elements.sent-2411-048: One way to assess how an LLM would behave in an interview setting offline is to perform a counterfactual simulation \cite{shaikh-etal-2024-grounding .}
elements.sent-2411-049: Specifically, given a human interview consisting of $n$ interviewer-source conversational turns $(q_1, a_1)...(q_n, a_n)$, we feed $t-1$ turns into the LLM along with a prompt instructing the LLM to generate the next question.
elements.sent-2411-050: This generates a counterfactual, $g_t$ to what the human would have said, $h_t$.
elements.sent-2411-051: We experiment with different variations: (1) Baseline: The LLM is simply asked to produce the next question.
elements.sent-2411-052: All question-generation experiments are conducted using Llama-3.1-70b.
elements.figure-2411-004: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{figures/splash-image.png} \caption{Walkthrough of the LLM Interviewer-Agent Process. To set up the interview, the interviewer agent is given a set of high-level objectives, similar to a journalist’s pre-interview notes, while the source is given a persona and a set of relevant facts. In each turn, the interviewer asks a question (Step 1). The source determines what information to reveal based on relevance and comfort level (Step 2a). Depending on the source's comfort, a subset of relevant information is randomly selected for the response (Step 2b). The source then crafts a reply aligned with their persona (Step 2c). The reward given to the interview agent, at the end of $k$ turns, is the number of information items extracted from the source.} \label{fig:splash_image}
elements.sent-2411-053: To analyze how similar LLM questions, $g_t$ are to human questions, $h_t$, we perform two analyses:.
elements.sent-2411-054: We aim to assess how similar $g_t$ is to $h_t$ across different comparison categories \newcite{saha2024branch , specifically: Informational consistency (i.e. $g_t$ and $h_t$ seek similar informational objectives); Motivational, (i.e. similar outcomes); Style, (i.e. similar tone); Contextual consistency (i.e. similar appropriateness given the context); Discourse consistency (i.e. similar purposes in the overall conversation).}
elements.sent-2411-055: Putting these together, we assess an $Exact$ match.
elements.sent-2411-056: We ask an LLM, GPT-4o, to perform this assessment and manually inspect its outputs and reasoning threads.
elements.sent-2411-057: We aim to assess whether $g_t$ plays a similar function as $h_t$ does.
elements.sent-2411-058: We develop a schema to describe the role of each question\footnote{To generate our discourse schema, we asked two journalists to analyze 50 interview transcripts.}
elements.sent-2411-059: One had 8 years of experience in newsrooms, the other was an undergraduate student studying journalism.
elements.sent-2411-060: We held three conferencing sessions to develop the schema.
elements.sent-2411-061: Then, we blindly annotated 10 interviews, achieving a $\kappa=.6$.
elements.sent-2411-062: Given our schema, we then asked an LLM to classify discourse roles in sentences.
elements.sent-2411-063: The prompt contains the interview context, $(q_1, a_1)...(q_{t-1 , a_{t-1 )$, and current question $q_t$.}}
elements.sent-2411-064: To validate the LLM's labeling accuracy, we had the professional journalist label 10 additional interviews as ground-truth and scored the LLM's assignments.
elements.sent-2411-065: The LLM scored a $.8$ f1 score.. .
elements.sent-2411-066: This schema includes the following elements: Follow-up Question (e.g. ``Can you tell us more?''), Outline-Level Question (e.g. ``Moving on, can we discuss the next event?''), Acknowledgement Statement (e.g., ``I see, that sounds scary.''), Opinion/Speculation (e.g. ``What do you think will happen?''), Broadening Question (e.g. ``How does this fit into the broader trend?''), Verification Question (e.g. `` So to confirm...'') and Challenge Question (e.g. ``These dates don't line up.'').
elements.sent-2411-067: See Table~\ref{tab:discourse-types in the Appendix for definitions of each discourse role.}
elements.sent-2411-068: Our analysis yielded several key findings.
elements.acknowledgements-2411-001: Insight \#1: Acknowledgement statements are virtually absent from all LLM variations.
elements.sent-2411-069: As shown in Figure \ref{fig:discoure_pie_charts , grounding gaps exist in journalistic interviewing similar to those observed by \cite{shaikh2024grounding .}}
elements.sent-2411-070: While human journalistic interviewers tend to make Acknowledgement statements in about 9\% of their utterances, all prompting variations that we experimented with made close to zero acknowledging statements.
elements.sent-2411-071: This lack of acknowledgement is paired with not mirroring the source's speaking style; human journalists, as shown in Appendix \ref{app:interview_examples , bring character and voice.}
elements.paragraph-2411-003: Insight \#2: LLMs do not engage in strategic multi-turn questioning
elements.sent-2411-072: Even in settings where LLMs are exposed to interview outlines, they are still undirected in their questions.
elements.sent-2411-073: As shown in Figure \ref{fig:discoure_pie_charts , LLMs are significantly more likely to ask follow-up questions than humans across all prompting variations.}
elements.sent-2411-074: Introducing chain-of-thought and outline variations increases the rate at which the LLM asks outline-level questions.
elements.sent-2411-075: However, the rate remains significantly below human levels.
elements.sent-2411-076: Additionally, they are also more likely to ask either Opinion questions or Broadening questions.
elements.sent-2411-077: In fact, in Figure \ref{fig:llm-discourse-across-time , we observe that LLMs tend to ask increasing amounts of Opinion Questions and Broadening Questions over time, which humans do not.}
elements.sent-2411-078: As can be seen in Table \ref{tab:llm_examples , these questions can be vague and open-ended and reflect a lack of planning.}
elements.sent-2411-079: Together, these findings suggest an inability to direct an interview in a desired direction and engage in multi-turn planning.
elements.paragraph-2411-004: Insight \#3: LLMs are capable of understanding context, but fail in all other categories of similarity to humans.
elements.sent-2411-080: Comparing the content and style of LLM interviews to human interviews in Table \ref{tab:consistency_evaluation , we note that, overall, LLMs are broadly dissimilar to humans in style, motivation and information-seeking.}
elements.sent-2411-081: One area where the LLMs succeed, relatively, is understanding the context of the interview beforehand.
elements.sent-2411-082: This is not a new observation -- much recent work, e.g. in dialogue-tracking, has found LLMs to perform well \cite{ou2024dialogbench .}
elements.sent-2411-083: The fact that LLMs can preserve context over multiple turns and do not drift away from the topic indicates that models might one day be able to engage in multi-turn goal-oriented dialogue, given the right reward signals and learning environment.
elements.sent-2411-084: Taken together, these findings suggest that journalistic dialogue is suitable for studying effective communication patterns, and also highlight significant gaps in current language modeling objectives.
elements.sent-2411-085: While LLMs can generate contextually relevant questions, they lack both an emotional and connective drive as well as the strategic planning exhibited by human interviewers.
elements.sent-2411-086: As shown, LLM counterfactual questions exhibit several shortcomings: they are less likely to acknowledge the interviewee and focus excessively on follow-up questions.
elements.sent-2411-087: However, questions remain: do they lack strategic multi-turn planning? In human dialogue, grounding exists ultimately strategic purposes \cite{cialdini2009influence .}
elements.sent-2411-088: Our goal for the remainder of the paper is to set up a realistic simulated game-environment with a delayed reward signal to test this.
elements.sent-2411-089: We first introduce our game on a high level, explain our game design, as illustrated in Figure~\ref{fig:splash_image , and then describe our implementation.}
elements.sent-2411-090: We draw on two journalism textbooks: Interviewing: A Guide for Journalists and Writers, which explains how to conduct effective interviews and speak to reluctant, defensive, or poor-explaining sources \cite{sedorkin2015interviewing ; and Journalism: Principles and Practice, which describes how to build source trust \cite{harcup2015journalism .}}
elements.sent-2411-091: Our gameplay proceeds in a loop, shown in Algorithm \ref{alg:gameplay .}
elements.sent-2411-092: The ``player'' in our game plays the role of an interviewer and is able to ask questions to a source, based on the conversational history and the interview objectives (the Interviewer() step).
elements.sent-2411-093: The source is given a set of informational items, and assesses whether any of these items are relevant to the question (the getRelevantInfo() step); they then decide how persuaded or comfortable they are based on the conversational history (the getPersuasionLevel() step); based on this, we determine the subset of relevant items they return (the getItemsToReturn()).
elements.sent-2411-095: The reward, obtained at the end of the game, is that amount of information items the source disclosed.
elements.sent-2411-096: We first start by describing our data processing, and then we will describe the functions introduced in Algorithm \ref{alg:gameplay in more detail.}
elements.algorithm-2411-001: \caption{Gameplay} \label{alg:gameplay} \hspace*{\algorithmicindent} Input Interviewer objectives $o$, Source Informational Items $I$, Source persona $\phi$, $K$ turns \hspace*{\algorithmicindent} Output Reward \( R \) \begin{algorithmic}[1] \State Initialize: Reward \( R \leftarrow 0 \), Conversation History $c\leftarrow \left[\right]$, Used items $u\leftarrow\{\}$ \For{i $\in 1,...K$} \Statex \(\triangleright\) Step 1: Interviewer Question Generation \State $q =$ Interviewer$(c, o)$ \Statex \(\triangleright\) Step 2: Source's Response Generation \State $r=$getRelevantInfoItems$(I, u, q)$ \State $p=$getPersuasionLevel$(c)$ \State $f=$getItemsToReturn$(r, p)$ \State $a=$Source$(q,c,f,p,\phi)$ \Statex \(\triangleright\) Update Variables \State $u \leftarrow u \cup f$, $c \leftarrow c \oplus \left[q, a\right]$, $R\leftarrow R + |f|$ \EndFor \end{algorithmic}
elements.sent-2411-097: To prepare our dataset for use in the simulated game environment, we group together: (1) source responses and ask an LLM\footnote{Llama-3.1-70b to summarize a set of specific informational items and (2) interviewer questions and ask an LLM to summarize them into a set of high-level objectives.}
elements.sent-2411-098: The sources' informational items mimic the knowledge a source likely had going into the interview\footnote{Manual evaluation confirms that these information items were present in the initial interview, are concise and non-overlapping.}
elements.sent-2411-099: Both of these summaries are represented in Figure \ref{fig:splash_image as Given, and are designed to give the interviewer-LLM and the source-LLM a basis for communication.}
elements.sent-2411-100: For further examples of both, see Tables \ref{table:info_items and \ref{tab:outline_objectives in the Appendix.}}
elements.sent-2411-101: Now, we introduce the design of the source.
elements.sent-2411-102: We focus attention on this construction to build a robust game environment that accurately mimics human interactions.
elements.sent-2411-103: To make gameplay varied and challenging, we draw from \citet{sedorkin2015interviewing to design eight different personas: Anxious, Avoidant, Adversarial, Defensive, Straightforward, Poor Explainer, Dominating and Clueless.}
elements.sent-2411-104: To see detailed descriptions of each persona, as well as example responses that the persona might give, see Table \ref{table:personas .}
elements.sent-2411-105: Different personas give us the ability to study how interviewers perform in a wider array of scenarios and are designed to more carefully capture different challenges that journalists face.
elements.figure-2411-005: \begin{tabular}{p{3.5cm}p{3.5cm}p{3.5cm}p{3.5cm}} {} Hardest Intermediate Easiest Model Full Game sans. Persuasion sans. Info. witholding gpt-4o-mini 49.3\% 47.5\% 84.7\% gpt-4o 50.4\% 49.8\% 84.2\% Llama-3.1-70b 42.6\% 45.5\% 80.1\% Llama-3.1-8b 42.4\% 48.3\% 74.9\% \end{tabular} \caption{Performance of LLMs as Interviewers, with Ablations Percentage of information items extracted (Reward \%) in each interview by different language models (gpt-4o-mini, gpt-4o, Llama-3.1-70b, and Llama-3.1-8b) across three conditions: (1) Hardest: The full game, with information dependent on persuasion and persona. (2) Medium: an ablation removing the sources' responsiveness to persuasion. (3) Easy: An ablation removing the random withholding of information (i.e. a source returns all relevant information items at each turn). We observe, perhaps unsurprisingly, that removing the source's ability to withhold information (Intermediate $\rightarrow$ Easy) drastically increases the reward \% at the end of the game. The removal of persuasion strategies has a smaller effect, with some models showing marginal gains (e.g., Llama-3.1-8b) and others slight losses (e.g., gpt-4o). This indicates that vanilla LLMs are poorly suited to this persuasion task.} \label{tab:results_of_gameplay}
elements.figure-2411-006: \begin{subfigure}{\linewidth} \includegraphics[width=.8\linewidth]{figures/extractions_by_persona.png} \caption{Rewards of gpt-4o from playing against sources of different persona types.} \label{fig:extractions-by-persona} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{\linewidth} \includegraphics[width=.8\linewidth]{figures/average_persuasion_by_persona.png} \caption{Average level of persuasion, from gpt-4o, towards the different persona types in our evaluation.} \label{fig:average-persuasion-by-persona} \end{subfigure} \caption{Comparison of gpt-4o's performance across different persona types: extraction rewards and persuasion levels. The Adversarial type is by far the hardest to extract information from, however, it is easier to persuade. This could be because the LLMs are the most thrown off by adversarial sources.} \label{fig:combined-figures-persona}
elements.sent-2411-106: The following three functions, in sequence, power our gameplay: $getRelevantInfoItems \rightarrow getPersuasionLevel \rightarrow getItemsToReturn$.
elements.sent-2411-107: The first, getRelevantInfoItems, takes the interviewer's question and determines which of the sources' information items are most relevant; it is simply a retrieval function that we implement using an LLM.
elements.sent-2411-108: The persuadability component to our gameplay increases the multi-turn strategy: because persuasion is assessed with reference to the entire interview, the interviewer gets more reward overall for spending words (or even whole turns) making the source feel comfortable early in the interview, rather than directly asking for information.
elements.sent-2411-109: However, is it sound for the source-LLM to assess its own level of persuasion? As recent research has found, LLMs are poor detectors of when they are being persuaded \cite{sakurai-miyao-2024-evaluating and can even unknowingly persuade themselves \cite{zeng2024johnny .}}
elements.sent-2411-110: Furthermore, persuadability varies from person to person \cite{wang2019persuasion,hirsh2012personalized .}
elements.sent-2411-111: Luckily, source-persuasion is a well-studied field in journalism.
elements.sent-2411-112: As a starting point, we draw from \citet{sedorkin2015interviewing , and carefully design prompts asking an LLM to rate the persuasiveness of a prior conversation.}
elements.sent-2411-113: Different source personas, according to \citet{sedorkin2015interviewing , are persuaded by different communication patterns: e.g. Anxious sources are distrustful of journalists; they are usually persuaded by phrases like ``I will be as fair as possible''.}
elements.sent-2411-114: We tailor different persuasive phrases for different simulated personas.
elements.sent-2411-115: We also experiment with varying lengths of interview context to include in the getPersuasionLevel prompt.
elements.sent-2411-116: For a full list of our persuasion approaches, see Table \ref{tab:persuasion_techniques .}
elements.sent-2411-117: We confirm the validity of LLM-identified persuasion levels by setting up human trials.
elements.sent-2411-118: In these trials, the human plays as a source.
elements.sent-2411-119: The human receives source information items and is asked questions by the interviewer.
elements.sent-2411-120: After each question, the human rates the their own level of persuasiveness on a 5 point scale and we calculate the agreement between the human and the source.
elements.sent-2411-121: After 72 trials, we report $r=.43$ at $p<.0001$, a statistically significant moderate correlation (if we exclude ``Adversarial Sources'', our correlation is $r=.68$ at $p<1e9$, indicating that persuasiveness evaluation in this context is relatively objective).
elements.sent-2411-122: With these steps, we can simulate persuadability relatively well, on a turn level.
elements.sent-2411-123: We invite future work on more realistic simulation methods.
elements.figure-2411-007: \begin{subfigure}{0.8\linewidth} \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{figures/extractions_over_time.png} \caption{Average reward across conversational turns.} \label{fig:extractions-over-time} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{0.8\linewidth} \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{figures/total_extractions_over_time.png} \caption{Percentage (\%) of Reward, by total reward, over time.} \label{fig:total-extractions-over-time} \end{subfigure} \caption{ Comparison of Rewards over time for language models. For all language models, the reward declines over time, shown above. However, this is not due to interviewer ``maxing out'' reward, as Total Reward increases nearly linearly across conversational turns.} \label{fig:combined-figures}
elements.sent-2411-124: Finally, based on the assessed persuasion level (1--5) of the conversation, we implement getItemsToReturn.
elements.sent-2411-125: This function takes in all relevant information items and randomly draws from a Beta distribution to determine what percentage of relevant information items to return.
elements.sent-2411-126: We choose 5 different parameterizations per persona, each corresponding to a different persuasion level.
elements.sent-2411-127: As can be seen in Figure \ref{fig:splash_image , we choose these parameterizations such that the more persuaded a source is, the more left-skewed the distribution is.}
elements.sent-2411-128: Each persona has a slightly different parameterization, reflecting that some personas need less persuasion (e.g. ``Dominant'') while others do not drastically change how much information they return even with more persuasion (e.g. poor explainer).
elements.sent-2411-129: See Figure \ref{fig:beta_distributions in the Appendix for the Beta distributions for each source.}
elements.sent-2411-130: To implement the conversational components of our game, we stick with a minimalist setup, as LLMs have been well-observed to be proficient at basic dialogue given sufficient structure \cite{ou2024dialogbench .}
elements.sent-2411-131: For Source, we provide the source with the information items to return, a description of its persona, and its prior assessed comfort/persuasion level.
elements.sent-2411-132: For Interviewer, we implement a prompting-based setup and leave more involved designs for future.
elements.sent-2411-133: We run our simulation for 200 interviews with four models as the interviewer: gpt-4o, gpt-4o-mini, Llama-3.1-70b and Llama-3.1-8b.
elements.sent-2411-134: For a source LLM, we used gpt-4o across all personas.
elements.sent-2411-135: Table \ref{tab:results_of_gameplay compares the performance of LLMs across three conditions: the full game, a version without persuasion, and a version where sources do not withhold information.}
elements.sent-2411-136: In the full game, where sources' responsiveness depends on persuasion and persona, the gpt-4o model performs the best, at 50.4\%.
elements.sent-2411-137: However, when persuasion is removed, performance only marginally improves across all models (e.g., Llama-3.1-70b reaches 45.5\%, while gpt-4o remains stable at 49.8\%), indicating that other aspects of the game (i.e. inferring which information the source has witheld) also pose a challenge.
elements.sent-2411-138: In the easiest condition, where no information withholding occurs, all models perform significantly better, with reward percentages reaching over 80\%, showing that withholding strategies are a major obstacle for current LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-139: Figure \ref{fig:extractions-by-persona highlights the performance of gpt-4o across different source personas.}
elements.sent-2411-140: The model achieves the highest information extraction from straightforward personas, while adversarial and defensive personas are the most challenging.
elements.sent-2411-141: Despite being harder to extract information from, adversarial sources are easier to persuade, as shown in Figure \ref{fig:average-persuasion-by-persona .}
elements.sent-2411-142: Figure \ref{fig:extractions-over-time explores how the reward (information extraction) changes over the course of an interview.}
elements.sent-2411-143: The results show a declining trend in reward per conversational turn.
elements.sent-2411-144: However, the total reward accumulated over time (Figure \ref{fig:total-extractions-over-time ) increasesalmost linearly, showing that the LLMs continue to extract information, albeit at a slower rate.}
elements.sent-2411-145: Together, these findings highlight the limitations of current LLMs in engaging with persuasive and strategic multi-turn interviews.
elements.sent-2411-146: While larger models like gpt-4o outperform smaller ones, they still exhibit significant gaps in persuasion and adaptive questioning, particularly when dealing with difficult personas.
elements.sent-2411-147: Our results from Sections \ref{sct:analysis_results and \ref{sct:results , taken together, show that news interview transcripts are a valuable and real-world dataset for learning about persuasion, grounding dialogue and multi-turn strategy.}}
elements.sent-2411-148: Our work builds off previous work identifying grounding gaps in language models \cite{shaikh2024grounding and an emerging thread in gameplay \cite{wongkamjan2024more, liu2024interintent , by extending both directions into the news domain, where real-world data is plentiful.}}
elements.sent-2411-149: In Section \ref{sct:analysis_results , we show that grounding persuasive dialogue exists in human interviewers, which LLMs fail to mimic.}
elements.sent-2411-150: In Section \ref{sct:results we show that LLMs struggle to extract information from sources embodying a variety of personas.}
elements.sent-2411-151: Interestingly, particular personas, like adversarial or avoidant sources, are noticeably more difficult for the LLM extract information from.
elements.sent-2411-152: Figure \ref{fig:average-persuasion-by-persona , for instance, might suggest that the LLM models are more responsive to engagement tactics when sources display hostility rather than indifference or avoidance.}
elements.sent-2411-153: In the context of prior work \cite{liu2024interintent, chawla2021casino , which tends to examine all interactions from a singular persona, these distinctions can point to further investigations illuminating why certain personas are worse than others.}
elements.sent-2411-154: The ultimate solution for a propensity towards multi-turn strategies and grounding language in LLMs, we feel, is to incorporate longer-range reward signals into the training process \cite{li2016deep .}
elements.sent-2411-155: After all, grounding exists for a purpose: to influence an outcome borne out over many conversational turns \cite{clark1996using, cialdini2009influence (e.g. in therapeutic environments, the patient is more open and makes more progress \cite{bohart1999clients ; in educational environments the student is more engaged and learns more \cite{brown1994guided ).}}}
elements.sent-2411-156: Our NewsInterview effort provides a game-playing framework to provide such a reward and test such strategies in a real-world scenario.
elements.sent-2411-157: This game-playing environment is complicated: throughout an interview, the LLM has to reason about what information it already has, what likely information the source has, and how to persuade the source to divulge this information.
elements.sent-2411-158: Each of these components is likely challenging for present-day LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-159: Figure \ref{fig:extractions-over-time suggests that LLMs struggle to maintain effective information extraction as the conversation progresses, possibly due to difficulties in adjusting their questioning strategies over time.}
elements.sent-2411-160: Furthermore, we see only marginal gains with large modeling sizes, suggesting that scale alone may not make meaningful progress.
elements.sent-2411-161: Despite the considerable challenges this game imposes, NewsInterview is still simpler than other game-playing environments: agent-based strategy games typically require the agent to develop a strategy while other players are also developing strategies \cite{chawla2021casino, liu2024interintent, wongkamjan2024more .}
elements.sent-2411-162: In NewsInterview, the source is not typically trying to persuade the journalist, simply to decide how much information to divulge.
elements.sent-2411-163: These components, taken together, suggest that NewsInterview is an ideal real-world starting place to start to incorporate multi-turn strategic dialogue from LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-164: We imagine that agents built off of this training environment will one day be able to: (1) identify persona characteristics easily in other game-players (2) develop strategy books, similar to those humans use \cite{sedorkin2015interviewing, harcup2015journalism (3) reason about early-vs-late stage conversational tactics.}
elements.sent-2411-165: These agents can not only help in practical tasks (e.g. by automating interviewing or by providing students an environment to learn in) but also give us more fundamental insights into the strategic approaches that work well.
elements.sent-2411-166: Our work has to be viewed with the following fundamental limitations.
elements.sent-2411-167: Despite observing high correlations between humans and LLM assessments of persuasion, there could be many common scenarios we do not observe.
elements.sent-2411-168: For instance, there may be pathological degenerate cases: if, for instance, getPersuasionLevel returns a persuasion level of $5$ for agents that say ``thank you'', then agents will learn to say this at every turn, and will not develop any other strategy.
elements.sent-2411-169: We implemented our personas using prompts outlining what different personas would say and what they would be persuaded by, as described in Section \ref{sct:gameplay_design , under the expectation that the source-LLM would be able to generalize.}
elements.sent-2411-170: Table \ref{tab:results_of_gameplay does indicate that the interviewer-agent did not simply overfit to certain words.}
elements.sent-2411-171: However, if, in future work, we observe collapsing interviewer-agent behavior especially in the presence of trained approaches, we must be prepared to do more work to validate or improve getPersuasionLevel.
elements.sent-2411-172: Additionally, future work could also focus on introducing additional reward signals into the game-play.
elements.sent-2411-173: Some pieces or information are more important than others.
elements.sent-2411-174: By analyzing how much time is spent discussing each of a source's pieces of information, we might reward the agent more for the more important extractions.
elements.sent-2411-175: Another reward signal could be the quotability of the response.
elements.sent-2411-176: Often, an interviewer will seek to obtain specific, memorable lines to write about throughout the interview.
elements.sent-2411-177: There is line of research that tries to predict which parts of a text or speech are the most quotable \cite{bendersky2012dictionary, lee2016quote, maclaughlin2018predicting .}
elements.sent-2411-178: We believe both of these signals might be an interesting lens through which to improve NewsInterview.
elements.sent-2411-179: Recent research on large language models (LLMs) has highlighted a lack of grounding language and strategic dialogue \cite{shaikh2024grounding, wongkamjan2024more .}
elements.sent-2411-180: Grounding communication, which involves the use of acknowledgments and affirmations to foster understanding and trust \cite{clark1996using, cho-may-2020-grounding , is essential in various conversational settings such as education \cite{kasneci2023chatgpt , mental health \cite{carlbring2023new , and conflict resolution \cite{argyle2023ai .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-181: Prior efforts to study and improve LLMs' grounding dialogue capabilities have faced limitations due to the scarcity of large-scale, naturalistic datasets \cite{kasneci2023chatgpt .}
elements.sent-2411-182: Existing datasets are either generated via crowdsourcing, which can result in unnatural dialogues \cite{rashkin-etal-2019-towards, wang2019persuasion, liu2021towards , or are small-scale due to privacy concerns, as in educational \cite{caines2020teacher and therapeutic settings \cite{gratch-etal-2014-distress, casey2004challenges .}}}
elements.sent-2411-183: Grounding dialogue exists to foster longer-term goals.
elements.sent-2411-184: LLMs have been shown to lack multi-turn strategic thinking and planning, which are critical for effective dialogue \cite{chawla2023social .}
elements.sent-2411-185: While some studies have explored the use of game-playing environments to improve these aspects \cite{li2016deep, wongkamjan2024more , and the development of `gamified' persona-based subjects has a long history in dialogue agent research \cite{Colby_1981 , there is still a need for realistic datasets and simulations that can facilitate the development of agents with longer-horizon rewards.}}
elements.sent-2411-186: Research has increasingly focused on using game-playing environments to train agents in strategic dialogue.
elements.sent-2411-187: Similarly, \citet{he2018decoupling proposed a decoupled policy optimization approach for strategic dialogues in games.}
elements.sent-2411-188: More recent works have extended strategic dialogue to complex, multi-agent environments.
elements.sent-2411-189: These works highlight the effectiveness of simulated game environments in training agents for strategic multi-turn dialogues, underscoring the potential of such approaches in enhancing LLMs' strategic dialogue capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-190: Despite these advancements, several gaps remain.
elements.sent-2411-191: Most existing models focus on task-specific dialogues with singular personas and lack the flexibility to handle a variety of conversational contexts and topics.
elements.sent-2411-192: Another gap is the scarcity of large-scale, naturalistic datasets to create a broader variety of settings for these games, hampering the development of more generalized and robust models \cite{liu2021towards .}
elements.sent-2411-193: Addressing these gaps is crucial for advancing the development of agents capable of engaging in sophisticated strategic dialogues akin to human interactions.
elements.sent-2411-194: Our work addresses these gaps by introducing a large-scale dataset of 40,000 two-person informational interviews from NPR and CNN, providing a rich resource for studying grounding communication in a naturalistic setting.
elements.sent-2411-195: Additionally, we develop a realistic simulated environment, NewsInterview, which incorporates source personas and persuasive elements to evaluate and improve LLMs' strategic dialogue capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-196: In this paper, we have introduced a high-quality dataset of 40,000 two-person informational interviews from NPR and CNN, addressing the scarcity of large-scale dialogue data necessary for studying grounding communication.
elements.sent-2411-197: Our detailed discourse analysis reveals significant differences between LLM-generated dialogues and human interviewers, particularly in the use of grounding language and question types.
elements.sent-2411-198: Motivated by observation that long-term objectives guide turn-level grounding, we develop a realistic game environment, NewsInterview, to test and improve dialogue agents in informational interviews.
elements.sent-2411-199: Our experiments demonstrate that while source LLMs can mimic human behavior in information sharing, interviewer LLMs struggle with recognizing when questions are answered and engaging persuasively, leading to suboptimal information extraction.
elements.sent-2411-200: These findings underscore the need for enhancing LLMs' strategic dialogue capabilities, and we believe that our dataset and simulation environment provide valuable resources for future research in this area.
elements.sent-2411-201: All data used in this study are publicly available and do not contain personally identifiable information beyond what has been already made public by the news organizations.
elements.sent-2411-202: We adhere to ethical guidelines for data use and ensure that our processing respects the rights and privacy of individuals involved as well as the news organizations that collected this data.
elements.sent-2411-203: Since the dataset we create is derived from interviews that have already been published in academic settings, we believe we are infringing upon the copyright of the news organizations this data originally belonged it.
elements.sent-2411-204: Aside from ownership questions, there are still inherent risks in the use of real-world interview data.
elements.sent-2411-205: Some interviews might involve sensitive topics, and the ethical implications of using such data for model evaluation warrant careful consideration.
elements.sent-2411-206: All experiments are conducted using publicly available models and datasets.
elements.sent-2411-207: Part of our simulation does rely on high-performing language models and to serve this we used gpt-4o.
elements.sent-2411-208: This brings us into territory where we are inherently not reproducible, as closed models can be changed without notice.
elements.sent-2411-209: However, we believe we are not out of the norm in the academic community in our usage.
elements.sent-2411-210: The simulated game-playing environment used to evaluate the LLM agents is a simplification of real-world interviewing processes.
elements.sent-2411-211: We might be inducing a bias in agents that could perpetrate and ultimately lead development in the wrong direction.
elements.sent-2411-212: Or, we also might be opening up a sandbox for potential dual-use.
elements.sent-2411-213: The design of our game, to extract information from sources, might one day be used to persuade users to divulge sensitive information.
elements.sent-2411-214: We worked with multiple professional journalists throughout the summer who were either colleagues or students who signed up to work with us.
elements.sent-2411-215: They were all volunteering their time and efforts to help with the research.
elements.sent-2411-216: All experiments were run either with OpenAI resources (we spent a total of $\$300$ running simulations) or open source Llama-3.1-70b models.
elements.sent-2411-217: These models were run on the university cluster, which consisted of either 4xA40s or 2xA100s NVIDIA GPUS..
elements.figure-2411-008: \begin{tabular}{p{1.5cm}p{5.5cm}p{7.5cm}} Persona Description Example Responses Anxious Unsure if they should be doing the interview, often expresses doubt. "I'm not sure if I should be saying this, I should speak to my manager." Avoidant Brief, deflects questions, avoids detail, and changes subjects. "Actually, one of the main issues was the supply chain, but we've sorted that out." Adversarial Hostile, challenges the interviewer, provides confrontational replies. "Maybe if you did your job well, you'd understand the data. I'm not here to educate you." Defensive Protects reputation, feels criticized, gives overly detailed explanations. "One area where costs increased was in material prices, which were out of our control." Straight- forward Clear, direct, and willing to provide detailed information. "Additionally, we ran out funding midway through the project." Poor Explainer Struggles to explain clearly, rambles, or provides convoluted answers. "Uh, well, I guess the supply chain was part of it, but, uh, that's only one part of the story..." Dominating Controls the conversation, gives lengthy or off-topic answers. "Costs were high, but at my suggestion we brought in the best experts worldwide." Clueless Confused and uncertain, often unsure of the topic. "Oh, right, the delays... yeah, maybe it was the, uh, supply issues? I’m not too sure..." \end{tabular} \caption{Source Personas that we created, with Descriptions and Example Responses} \label{table:personas}
elements.figure-2411-009: \begin{tabular}{p{0.1\linewidth} p{0.41\linewidth} p{0.41\linewidth}} Persona Persuasion Description Persuasion Examples Anxious Responds well to empathetic, reassuring, and patient conversations. Encouraging, non-threatening language builds comfort. "I will be as fair as possible.", "I appreciate your honesty.", "If you're not comfortable now, I can come back later." Avoidant Prefers non-obtrusive small talk, short questions, and space to respond. Open-ended, light prompts work well. "And that happened when?", "I imagine there's more to the story.", "Ah I see." Adversarial Responds to thorough research, persistence, and fact-based questions. Repeated questioning elicits responses. "Our records indicate...", "Just to be clear, are you saying...?", "Earlier you stated..." Defensive Engages with non-confrontational and validating conversations. Neutral language reduces defensiveness. "I see why you made that choice.", "We can work together.", "It's understandable." Straight-forward Prefers direct and transparent conversations. Efficiency and brevity are key. "Let's get to the solution.", "What were the key points, in your view?" Poor Explainer Responds well to structured, patient conversations. Simple clarifying questions and validation help communication. "Explain that part again in smaller steps.", "I understand, keep going.", "Take your time." Dominating Engages when their expertise is acknowledged. Validation and offering control builds rapport. "I'd love your take.", "You have experience, what do you suggest?", "Your insights are valuable." Clueless Guided, simple questions with firm direction are effective. Breaking down complex topics increases confidence. "Tell me what you're thinking.", "It's okay to be unsure.", "Start with something simple." \end{tabular} \caption{Persuasion techniques that we compiled for different sources types. These manners and styles of speaking were informed by examples given in \newcite{harcup2015journalism} and \newcite{sedorkin2015interviewing} that sources with different personality types find the most persuasive.} \label{tab:persuasion_techniques}
elements.figure-2411-010: \begin{tabular}{p{.2\textwidth}p{0.7\textwidth}} Discourse Role Definition \hangindent=.5cm Starting/Ending Remarks \hangindent=.5cm Initiates or concludes the interview. Often not in the form of a question. [.5em] \hangindent=.5cm Acknowledgement Statement \hangindent=.5cm Affirms the interviewee, often by explicitly recognizing their previous response. Builds rapport, demonstrates active listening. Typically induces the source to engage in greater openness. [.5em] \hangindent=.5cm Follow-Up Question \hangindent=.5cm Digs deeper into a topic, seeks elaboration, or re-phrases a previous question to keep the discussion focused. [.5em] \hangindent=.5cm Verification Question \hangindent=.5cm Confirms the accuracy of a statement, fact, event or observation or assumption. [.5em] \hangindent=.5cm Topic-Transition Question \hangindent=.5cm Shifts the conversation to a new subject, usually an outline-level goal that the journalist prepared before the interview. [.5em] \hangindent=.5cm Opinion/Speculation Question \hangindent=.5cm Solicits the interviewee’s views or predictions, revealing biases or insights. [.5em] \hangindent=.5cm Challenge Question \hangindent=.5cm Tests the interviewee’s position, argument, or credibility, often provoking thought or debate. [.5em] \hangindent=.5cm Broadening Question \hangindent=.5cm Expands the scope of discussion, encouraging the interviewee to consider broader contexts or new perspectives. \end{tabular} \caption{Discourse types in informational interviews. We developed these definitions manually between three annotators by examining 50 different interviews.} \label{tab:discourse-types}
elements.sent-2411-218: We give discourse definitions for our 8 discourse categories in Table \ref{tab:discourse-types .}
elements.sent-2411-219: We developed these definitions between 3 annotators, one of which was a former professional journalist, another was a journalist undergraduate student and the third was a computer science undergraduate student.
elements.sent-2411-220: We conferenced 3 times, examining over 50 interviews, sorting questions into categories, and expanding these categories until we were reliably able to label new questions.
elements.sent-2411-221: We calculated an inter-annotator agreement of $\kappa$ = .6 between the annotators on a shared set of 10 interviews.
elements.sent-2411-222: Then, we used an LLM, Llama-3.1-70b to label discourse on the entire interview.
elements.sent-2411-223: The former journalist manually evaluated the LLM's performance during a blind trial and had an agreement of $\kappa=.8$ with the LLM.
elements.figure-2411-011: \begin{tabular}{p{7cm}} Processed Outline Source biography: Senior news analyst with expertise in politics and history. Interview context: A president's final year in office and potential changes in policy. Objective 1: Presidential legacy Objective 2: Foreign policy shifts Objective 3: Domestic policy changes Objective 4: Potential surprises \end{tabular} \caption{Interview Outline Objectives given to an interviewing game playing agent.} \label{tab:outline_objectives}

elements.figure-2411-012: \begin{tabular}{|p{14cm}|} Information Items Row 1: Information item \#1: The economy is growing above trend pace, with job growth of 150,000-200,000 a month, which is higher than what's sustainable in the long run. Row 2: Information item \#2: The Fed will likely continue to raise interest rates, as the economy is growing and financial conditions are still very accommodative. Row 3: Information item \#3: The neutral rate is probably higher than where we are right now, but it's not a precise number, and the Fed needs to curtail monetary policy further. Row 4: Information item \#4: The dot-plot is just a forecast and should not be taken as a commitment; it's subject to change as new information becomes available. Row 5: Information item \#5: The stock market will likely face tougher going in 2019, with slower earnings growth and higher interest rates. Row 6: Information item \#6: The economy's capacity to continue growing is a concern, as there aren't enough workers to sustain above-trend growth for more than another year or so. \end{tabular} \caption{Information Items for a source in our game-playing environment, extracted from an interview featuring Bill Dudley, Former President of the New York Federal Reserve} \label{table:info_items}

elements.figure-2411-013: \begin{tabular}{|p{14.5cm}|} LLM-Generated Counterfactual Questions What do you think about the changing dynamics of your neighborhood and how it affects your sense of community and belonging? What specific factors do you think are contributing to the increasing rates of HIV/AIDS among African-American women in Washington D.C.? How do parents with HIV/AIDS typically cope with the fear of not being there for their children, and what are the emotional and psychological implications of this fear on their mental health? What specific steps are you taking to mitigate the impact of the Salmonella outbreak on your business, and do you think this will have a lasting effect on the tomato industry as a whole? What about the potential impact of this rate drop on the overall housing market, and do you think it could lead to a rebound in housing prices or a continued decline? What are some practical steps parents can take to help their teenagers prepare for the job market and make the most of their summer? \end{tabular} \caption{List of Interview Questions generated in a counterfactual setting by LLM interviewer. The questions are generated after observing the previous $t$ human conversational turns.} \label{tab:llm_examples}

elements.figure-2411-014: \begin{subfigure}{0.45\textwidth} \includegraphics[height=0.12\textheight]{figures/beta_dist_anxious.png} \caption{Anxious} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{0.45\textwidth} \includegraphics[height=0.12\textheight]{figures/beta_dist_straightforward.png} \caption{Straightforward} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{0.45\textwidth} \includegraphics[height=0.12\textheight]{figures/beta_dist_poor_explainer.png} \caption{Poor Explainer} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{0.45\textwidth} \includegraphics[height=0.12\textheight]{figures/beta_dist_clueless.png} \caption{Clueless} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{0.45\textwidth} \includegraphics[height=0.12\textheight]{figures/beta_dist_dominating.png} \caption{Dominating} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{0.45\textwidth} \includegraphics[height=0.12\textheight]{figures/beta_dist_avoidant.png} \caption{Avoidant} \end{subfigure} \caption{Beta Distributions for Various Interview Personas} \label{fig:beta_distributions}
elements.sent-2411-242: NPR-85 FARAI CHIDEYA, host: Tony, I guess there will always be some kind of history made every day.
elements.sent-2411-243: TONY COX, host: You know, some of it good.
elements.sent-2411-244: Some of it, not so good.
elements.sent-2411-245: FARAI CHIDEYA, host: And while some of it is well-publicized, sometimes, notable history goes under the radar.
elements.sent-2411-246: TONY COX, host: Now, that's true.
elements.sent-2411-247: FARAI CHIDEYA, host: I'm thinking of your interview with Mable John.
elements.sent-2411-249: Now, this is a woman with an interesting past.
elements.sent-2411-250: Ms. MABLE JOHN (Singer): (Singing) My name is Mable and don't you think I ain't able.
elements.sent-2411-251: TONY COX, host: The 77-year-old Louisiana native has been a top R\&B singer, a successful novelist, a pastor, an activist and a movie actor, and I found out that Mable John is full of stories like the one about the time she met record mogul Berry Gordy before Motown was even Motown.
elements.sent-2411-252: Ms. MABLE JOHN (Singer): (Singing) That you're leaving.
elements.sent-2411-253: Ms. MABLE JOHN (Singer): How I met Berry? That was at a barber shop on (unintelligible) that was near the fine show bar, and at that time men were wearing process.
elements.sent-2411-255: And I was dating a guy that was one of those process operators in the Chesterfield lounge and barbershop, and Berry was coming and getting his hair done.
elements.sent-2411-256: I was coaching choirs for my church.
elements.sent-2411-257: And my boyfriend introduced me to Berry Gordy because Berry said he was a songwriter and he was going to have a lot of people recording his songs.
elements.sent-2411-258: And my boyfriend said you need to stop doing all of this work for the church free, and that Berry Gordy do something with you so you can get paid.
elements.sent-2411-259: So he introduced me to Berry Gordy.
elements.sent-2411-260: TONY COX, host: Now, tell us the story.
elements.sent-2411-261: We're going to skip around a little bit.
elements.sent-2411-263: TONY COX, host: When you and Berry Gordy connected, as Motown was just becoming a company, a record company, you are the first female to record on a label, the Tamla label.
elements.sent-2411-266: Ms. MABLE JOHN (Singer): The first single female artist, because Claudette Robinson was a part of what become the Miracles, and he was managing them along with me.
elements.sent-2411-268: Ms. MABLE JOHN (Singer): So I was the first single female artist to be signed to Tamla, which is a part of the Motown family.
elements.sent-2411-269: TONY COX, host: When you think about that now, how do you feel about looking at that as a historic moment? Ms. MABLE JOHN (Singer): No one could have bought that time.
elements.sent-2411-270: God had to give it to me.
elements.sent-2411-272: TONY COX, host: I understand that you were rehearsing one day and these three young girls came in and interrupted your rehearsal.
elements.sent-2411-273: Ms. MABLE JOHN (Singer): The girls that we know now as The Supremes.
elements.sent-2411-274: They came into a rehearsal that I was doing with Berry Gordy because he played also for me, played piano for me.
elements.sent-2411-275: We were there rehearsing and these girls came in and I didn't quite remember everything that was said that day because it's been so long.
elements.sent-2411-276: But Mary Wilson of The Supremes, remembered when she was writing her book to say that when she first walked into Motown, the three of them walked in and my question to Berry Gordy was, why are they walking in on my rehearsal, because all of our rehearsals were private.
elements.sent-2411-277: Ms. MABLE JOHN (Singer): (Singing) It takes a more than 'em flashy old money and I wink from the corner of your eye.
elements.sent-2411-278: I don't want no big line calls, (unintelligible) caviar.
elements.sent-2411-279: Oh no, true love baby can be found 'cause you take a look around.
elements.sent-2411-280: TONY COX, host: Talking about faith.
elements.sent-2411-281: Your career at Motown never really took off, and after some few years, you decided to go to Memphis, where you joined the Stax label and hooked up with Porter and Isaac Hayes.
elements.sent-2411-282: And then, it was long after that that you had a million seller.
elements.sent-2411-284: Well, Motown, Berry Gordy, they were all along with God and my parents a part of my future.
elements.sent-2411-286: It was one that was different from everywhere else I've ever been.
elements.sent-2411-287: But I think it was a necessary one to make the transition for me from Motown to Stax.
elements.sent-2411-288: TONY COX, host: Now, your big song at Stax, one of your - the biggest of your songs was… Ms. MABLE JOHN (Singer): The biggest of all songs.
elements.sent-2411-289: TONY COX, host: "Your Good Thing is About to End." Ms. MABLE JOHN (Singer): "…Is About to End." Right.. Right.
elements.sent-2411-290: TONY COX, host: See, I'm old enough to have remembered that song.
elements.sent-2411-291: Ms. MABLE JOHN (Singer): Well, that's good.
elements.sent-2411-292: That makes me feel you don't have to be very old to remember that.
elements.sent-2411-293: Ms. MABLE JOHN (Singer): (Singing) I don't have to beg you to hold me 'cause somebody else will.
elements.sent-2411-294: You don't have to love me when I want it, 'cause somebody else will.
elements.sent-2411-295: Ms. MABLE JOHN (Singer): It was a story that I needed to tell because of a bad marriage.
elements.sent-2411-296: And at Stax, they would allow you to be yourself.
elements.sent-2411-297: Everybody participated in whatever success you're going to have, everybody, including the drummer.
elements.sent-2411-298: TONY COX, host: Really? Tell me about your family.
elements.sent-2411-299: And I'm switching to that for a reason because you were one of 10 children, right? Ms. MABLE JOHN (Singer): The oldest...
elements.sent-2411-300: TONY COX, host: The oldest of 10.
elements.sent-2411-302: TONY COX, host: And you happen to have a little brother, a baby brother who was a big time performer, Little Willie John.
elements.sent-2411-303: Ms. MABLE JOHN (Singer): Yes.. Little Willie John.
elements.sent-2411-305: Now, when I got with Willy that was another education.
elements.sent-2411-306: Ms. MABLE JOHN (Singer): Because he said my name is Little Willie John.
elements.sent-2411-307: It might be William Edward John to you, and you're my sister and I love you.
elements.sent-2411-308: But if you're not good, I'm going to send you home.
elements.sent-2411-309: TONY COX, host: Obviously, you are good.
elements.sent-2411-310: Ms. MABLE JOHN (Singer): Well, he let me stay.
elements.sent-2411-311: Ms. MABLE JOHN (Singer): (Singing) You have all the love that I've got.
elements.sent-2411-312: Even ice melts to water and gets hot.
elements.sent-2411-313: Look out, your good thing is about to come to an end.
elements.sent-2411-314: Your real good thing… TONY COX, host: You were the leader of the Raelettes for a dozen years.
elements.sent-2411-315: TONY COX, host: Traveling all over with and without Ray Charles.
elements.sent-2411-316: Ms. MABLE JOHN (Singer): With and without Ray Charles.. Yes.
elements.sent-2411-317: TONY COX, host: In the movie, "Ray," I had looked in the credits to see if there were someone who played you… Ms. MABLE JOHN (Singer): No. TONY COX, host: ...since you have been a Raelette for so long, and I saw that there wasn't one.
elements.sent-2411-318: Ms. MABLE JOHN (Singer): No. TONY COX, host: And is there a reason for that? Ms. MABLE JOHN (Singer): Well, it was the years before I came.
elements.sent-2411-320: Ms. MABLE JOHN (Singer): And I tell everybody that asks me, the best of his life were the years after the movie.
elements.sent-2411-321: When I came to work with him, he sat me down and told me all about his beginning, told me all about things that ticks him off and things that excite him, what he was looking for and how he wanted it.
elements.sent-2411-322: And I knew that being with him would finish me in this industry… TONY COX, host: Now, when he...
elements.sent-2411-323: Ms. MABLE JOHN (Singer): ...because he was at the top - complete me.
elements.sent-2411-324: Ms. MABLE JOHN (Singer): So that I could work for any audience, sing any kind of songs.
elements.sent-2411-325: Remember now, at the beginning I thought I could only sing gospel.
elements.sent-2411-326: With Berry Gordy, I found out I could sing the blues.
elements.sent-2411-327: I went to Stax and I find out I could sing love songs.
elements.sent-2411-328: I got with Ray Charles and we sang country - everything.
elements.sent-2411-329: And we could play to any audience.
elements.sent-2411-330: I wanted to sing what was in my heart to everybody that loves music, and Ray Charles was the place for me to be, to do that.
elements.sent-2411-331: TONY COX, host: So the Raelettes - would you say that was the highlight of your career? Ms. MABLE JOHN (Singer): It was a highlight.
elements.sent-2411-332: It was highlight because I learned things about myself, about my career, about the industry.
elements.sent-2411-333: I was able to set up my own publishing companies and production companies because of the knowledge that I gained with and from Ray Charles.
elements.sent-2411-334: TONY COX, host: And after all of that, Mable John, your career did not stop.
elements.sent-2411-335: It has gone on into movies, into - you've written a couple of novels.
elements.sent-2411-338: TONY COX, host: Oh, number three.
elements.sent-2411-339: You've done three novels.. You're a minister.
elements.sent-2411-340: TONY COX, host: And you started a church.
elements.sent-2411-341: TONY COX, host: And you help the homeless.
elements.sent-2411-342: TONY COX, host: And you're a grandmother.
elements.sent-2411-344: TONY COX, host: And a great-grandmother.
elements.sent-2411-345: How is it possible for one person to do all of those things and to do them as successfully as you have? Ms. MABLE JOHN (Singer): It's all God.
elements.sent-2411-346: Some days, when people are telling me how busy I am.
elements.sent-2411-347: And when I sit down to think about it, I get tired.
elements.sent-2411-348: Ms. MABLE JOHN (Singer): So I don't.
elements.sent-2411-350: I just get up every morning and I thank God for the activity of that day.
elements.sent-2411-351: And I have to thank a woman that's no longer with us, Ms. Billie Holiday, because that's the voice that I hear in my ear still to this day.
elements.sent-2411-352: I worked with her two weeks before she passed.
elements.sent-2411-353: And she said to me, Honey - because I was frightened out of my wits - you can make it if you remember.
elements.sent-2411-354: Always know when you have done or given enough.
elements.sent-2411-355: Not to be afraid and have guts enough to say I quit.
elements.sent-2411-356: Ms. MABLE JOHN (Singer): (Singing) Even ice melts to water and gets hot… TONY COX, host: It's so nice talking with you.
elements.sent-2411-358: Ms. MABLE JOHN (Singer): I thank you.
elements.sent-2411-359: Ms. MABLE JOHN (Singer): (Singing) Your good thing is about to come to an end.
elements.sent-2411-360: Your real good thing… FARAI CHIDEYA, host: That was NPR's Tony Cox with singer, author and actor Mable John.
elements.sent-2411-361: Look for Mable John in the upcoming John Sayles film, ``Honeydripper.'' Ms. MABLE JOHN (Singer): (Singing) Getting myself back together.
elements.sent-2411-362: FARAI CHIDEYA, host: That's our show for today, and thank you sharing your time with us.
elements.sent-2411-363: To listen to the show or subscribe to our podcast, visit our Web site, nprnewsandnotes.org.
elements.sent-2411-365: FARAI CHIDEYA, host: To join the conversation or sign up for our newsletter, visit our blog at nprnewsandviews.org.

elements.sent-2411-368: FARAI CHIDEYA, host: I'm Farai Chideya.
